title: Research
text:
Neurophysiological Markers of Existential Experience



Contemplative Neuroscience

EEG

Predictive Processing

Altered States



How does the brain process the awareness of mortality — and what happens when that processing shifts? At the Contemplative Neuroscience and Neurophenomenology Lab…










Tree Responses to Climatic Variability: Eco-Hydrological Modeling



Eco-Hydrology

Complex Systems

Climate

Dryland Ecology



How do trees respond to the interplay of soil moisture deficits and atmospheric drought — and what do long-term monitoring datasets reveal about adaptive capacity across…










Spatial Self-Organization and Resilience in Dryland Ecosystems



Pattern Formation

Dryland Ecology

Nonlinear Dynamics

Complex Systems



Dryland vegetation does not distribute itself randomly — it self-organizes into spatial patterns (spots, stripes, gaps) that function as a collective buffer against aridity.…










Spreading Phenomena on Complex Networks



Network Science

Epidemiology

Complex Systems

Computational Modeling



How does the topology of a social or biological network shape whether a perturbation cascades or gets absorbed? During my postdoctoral position at Tel Aviv University, I…










Knowledge Graphs and LLM Agents for Complex Domains



Knowledge Graphs

LLM Agents

Data Science

AI



Between academic positions, I worked as a data scientist and R&D lead building knowledge graph systems and LLM-based agentic pipelines across domains including precision…

title: Now
text:
This is a now page, where I occasionally update where I am, what I’m doing, and/or what I’m trying to focus on.


📍 Munich, Germany
👋 Goodbye Australia/2025, Hello Germany/2026!
I’m now three weeks into postdoc life at LMU Munich. Despite some minor road bumps, I’m settling in well:

My colleagues have all been super welcoming 🍻,
I have somewhere to live (until September at least…) 🏡,
I (think) all my paperwork is order ✔️,
I’ve found a regular yoga studio 🧘🏻‍♀️,
I had my first experience of walking to work in the snow! ❄️,
I had some hardware related ‘black swan’ moments – I learnt that the new M5 macbooks do not come with power adapters, and that when you ask for an “English” keyboard you might receive a British QWERTY keyboard with 105 keys, a short left shift key, and tall enter key, instead of the 104-key ANSI US English keyboard you were expecting 💻,
I learnt that in German, when ordering a filled sandwich – you should ask for “ham and egg” and not “egg and ham” 🔀.

On the research side, I’ve been thinking a lot about opportunities for collaboration and cross-pollination between Information Visualisation and Statistical Graphics research. Although both fields research the visualisation of data, and even shared some intellectual ancestry, the relationship between the two fields seems to be mostly coloured by confusion, talking past each other, and missed opportunities. During his opening keynote for IEEE VIS26, Hans-Christian Hege mused on the need for more principled visual arguments in InfoVis and SciVis. Statistics, and particularly the practice of constructing statistical arguments and inference, seem (to me) to be a perfect match for answering this call for an “argumentative basis” for visualisation interpretation. I’m not the first person to notice this, so why is this potential is untapped, and what could activate it?



📍 Melbourne, Australia
It’s official! As of Aug 20, 2025, I hold a doctoral degree in Mathematics and Statistics from Monash University. My thesis Unified Principles And Tools For Complex Datasets And Data-Driven Workflows was passed with minor revisions and is available online via Monash University’s Library.
Since my last update, I:

👩🏻‍🏫 Delivered a guest lecture, “LLMs for Preparing Data in R”, in the Wild Caught Data unit at Monash University
🧰 Attended the LMU-NYU-Workshop “Making Data Work: Tools for Better Statistical Practice” in Munich 
👩🏻‍🏫 Delivered my “Quarto for Economists” workshop for the third time!
🐻 Co-chaired WOMBAT2025 (Sep 29-30) – a two day conference bringing together data folks from industry, academia and government for tutorials and talks on this year’s theme of “Designing Data-Driven Discoveries”.
🇦🇹 Attended VIS2025 (Nov 1-7) in Vienna – ‘Out of the Loop: Enhancing Documentation and Transparency in Collaborative Causal Loop Diagrams to Capture Multiple Perspectives’
🐻 Co-organised the 2026 NUMBAT Hackathon,

Research wise, I’ve been thinking a lot about opportunities for dialogue between information visualisation and statistical graphics, while continuing to work on adapting LLMs for data cleaning.
Finally, I’m excited to be joining the Social Data Science and AI Lab at LMU Munich as a post-doctoral researcher from Jan, 2026!




📍 Melbourne, Australia
I’m finally (almost) PhD-done! I submitted my thesis for examination after a long but rewarding 3 years and 8 months. Although, I wish I had a bit more time to digest it all, I’m grateful to be leaving Melbourne shortly for a very exciting research trip to:

🇹🇼 Present recent work on an integrative framework for investigating and assessing web-scraped datasets for research applications at National Tsinghua University (Apr 14-18).
🇯🇵 Present ‘TableCanoniser: Interactive Grammar-Powered Transformation of Messy, Non-Relational Tables to Canonical Tables’ at CHI25 (Apr 27-May 1).

When I get back I’ll be continuing to work as a Research Fellow under the wonderful supervision of Rob J Hyndman on tools for ex-post harmonisation, and leveraging LLMs for data preparation.



📍 Vancouver, Canada, (updated Sep 2024)
I’m finished with conference travel for now and settling into Vancouver until late Oct. I added another conference to my trip (SmallDataSF – which was amazing!), and extended my stay in Vancouver through to the end of Nov. Still working on synthesising various definitions of provenance and provenance-related tools across computer science, applied statistics and data science.
I have some gripes with my past-self for over-packing my travel schedule, but really can’t complain about my first-time experiences at JSM, posit::conf(2024) and tidy-dev-day! I connected with so many amazing people in the data science and stats community, successfully delivered two new talks and even got to squeeze in a spot of outdoor climbing while in Seattle thanks to an old friend.



📍 Munich, Germany
After jumping through what felt like endless hoops of approval processes, funding changes and decision fatigue, I’ve finally started my many months long research trip across Europe & North America. Some upcoming highlights include:

🇩🇪 Visiting SODA group at LMU (Jun 13-26)
🇦🇹 Presenting on ex-post harmonisation and the {xmap} package at UseR!, Salzburg (Jul 8-11)
🇺🇸 Presenting on assessing statisical programming skills at JSM, Portland (Aug 3-9)
🇺🇸 Presenting on Quarto for Knowledge Management in the Level up your Data Science Skills session at posit::conf(2024), Seattle (Aug 12-14)
🇨🇦 Visiting the InfoVis group at UBC, Vancouver, (Jul-Nov)

It’s hard to express how grateful I am for this opportunity and the support I’ve gotten, especially from my supervisors, to not only expand my research network, but also to experience so many new cities and countries 🥰.
On great experiences, I may have found one of my favourite Vietnamese restaurants (ever, and that’s saying a lot coming from Melbourne) after getting caught in the rain in Amsterdam last week and striking up a conversation with the stranger I was sharing my table with at Saint Jean Deli. The sticky rice Xôi bowl at bonjour càphê is just the perfect blend of textural variety and flavour balance was just chef’s kiss. I had the fish & dill bowl, and the sticky rice felt amazingly light with just the right amount of bite, while the pickled mango gave the dish a sweet and acidic brightness that just perfectly tied together the slightly saltiness of the fish the aroma of the herbs and chilli. My only regret is that I only found this place on Monday when I was leaving the next morning, and their tasting menu is not available on Monday nights 🥲. I’m seriously considering when/how I might get myself back to Amsterdam to try it.

title: Sketchnote Page
text:
Cynthia Huang © 2022, CC BY-SA 4.0



Description of illustration/sketchnote:

This black and white illustration shows an iPad with a drawing app open to a sketchnote in the top left quadrant, which is linked by an arrow to a computer in the bottom left quadrant. The computer has three windows showing the logo of tools used to process a sketchnote: Inkscape, Quarto and file explorer. The computer is linked by an arrow to a webpage on the right side of the image with the title ‘How to Publish a Sketchnote’.

Links embedded in the graphic:

Inkscape
Quarto

link to media (podcast/book/media etc.)

Toggle the </> code menu to see .qmd file source code.

title: How to Pitch Your Best Ideas
text:
Cynthia Huang © 2022, CC BY-SA 4.0



Description of visual summary:

Black and white hand-drawn visual notes summarising the episode ‘How to Pitch Your Best Ideas’ from the podcast ‘WorkLife with Adam Grant’. The top half of the sketchnote illustrates three myths about making good elevator pitches. The bottom half summarises advice on making ongoing pitches. The key takeaway is written at the bottom of the sketchnote: ‘Remember, everyone want you to pitch them the best idea they’ve ever heard’

Links related to this visual summary:


WorkLife with Adam Grant: A TED original podcast
Episode Page
Episode Transcript

This visual summary was drawn with Notability in Sep 2022 and edited with Inkscape. (Cynthia Huang © 2022, CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Podcasts
text:
Episodes of The Random Sample hosted by yours truly:

EP 111: Bridging the interdisciplinary gap with Stats & Data Science – with Professor Galit Shmueli
EP 109: Designing the Tools that Shape Data Science – with Dr Hadley Wickham
EP 100: The Challenges of Publishing Interdisciplinary Research with Professor Xiao-Li Meng
EP 96: Scientific Graphic Design for Communicating Research with Dr Jess Hopf
EP 94: Understanding Math Struggles with Dr Jacob Paul
EP 87: What you need to know about Referendum Polling with Professor Simon Jackman
EP 86: Communicating Statistics & Probability with Professor Jeffrey Rosenthal
EP 81: Open-sourcing Data Science Education with Professor Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel
EP 79: Law & Stats: What’s a Statistician Doing in Court? with Professor Ian Gordon
EP 75: Probing Large Language Models & ChatGPT with Professor Simon Angus
EP 72: Future Proofing as Data Changes the World with Dana Ma
EP 70: The Statistical Society of Australia’s 60th Birthday with Ian Gordon and Jessica Kasza

and an episode where Professor Peter Taylor interviews me (and others) about being a regular host for the Random Sample:

EP 91: Why host a podcast?

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Physicist | Data Scientist | Researcher
Contact Information

LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/omer-tzuk/




Ph.D. in Physics of Complex Systems with expertise in developing algorithms for numerical analysis and mathematical modeling of physical systems. Over 12 years of experience in Python, data science, and machine learning, with a specialized focus on complex networks, knowledge graphs, and machine learning on graphs. Additional experience with retrieval augmented generation, large language models, and transformers. Currently conducting EEG research on consciousness and predictive processing.




Ph.D. in Physics of Complex Systems | 2014 – 2019 Ben-Gurion University of the Negev, Israel

Research: Dynamics of spatial pattern formation in complex systems.
Key Contributions: Developed algorithms for multidimensional stability analysis and numerical continuation; built Python codes for mathematical modeling and network-based population structure inference.

M.Sc. in Astrophysics | 2012 – 2014 University of Amsterdam, The Netherlands

Track: Gravitation and Astro-particle Physics excellence track.
Key Contributions: Wrote Python algorithms for Bayesian inference and Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods regarding dark matter interactions in the early universe.

B.Sc. in Astronomy and Astrophysics | 2008 – 2011 University of Bologna, Italy

Graduated with honors (con lode).





Postdoctoral Researcher | Oct 2025 – Present University of Haifa

Researcher at the Contemplative Neuroscience and Neurophenomenology Lab.
Conducting EEG data collection and analysis for studies on anxiety, altered states of consciousness, and predictive processing.
Named collaborator on NIH grant application.

LLM Engineer & Graph RAG Systems Architect | Dec 2024 – Sep 2025 BetterYou

Led R&D of knowledge graphs and graph machine learning for a psychophysiological AI assistant.

Researcher | Jul 2024 – Oct 2024 LAMBDA Lab, Tel Aviv University

Optimized Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines from Knowledge Graphs in collaboration with Alpha10X.

VP R&D | 2022 – Mar 2024 We-Agri Ltd.

Developed a carbon sequestration platform using LLMs and Knowledge Graphs.
Built geochemical simulation models in Python for soil organic carbon dynamics.

Researcher | 2022 – Jul 2022 DAOstack Ltd.

Developed smart contracts for on-chain parametric insurance products using Solidity on Ethereum.

Postdoctoral Researcher | 2019 – 2020 Tel-Aviv University

Built Python code to simulate large-scale hybrid epidemiological models on networks.

Research Assistant | 2013 CERN, Geneva, Switzerland

Developed a Bayesian inference tool in C++ (ROOT) for big data at the LHCb experiment.






Can Spatial Self-Organization Inhibit Evolutionary Adaptation? (2025) Bera, B. K., Tzuk, O., Bennett, J. J. R., et al. Journal of The Royal Society Interface, 22(222). DOI
Dryland Mechanisms Could Widely Control Ecosystem Functioning in a Drier and Warmer World (2022) Grünzweig, J. M., et al. Nat Ecol Evol, 6(8), 1064–1076. DOI
Linking Spatial Self-Organization to Community Assembly and Biodiversity (2021) Bera, B. K., Tzuk, O., Bennett, J. J., and Meron, E. eLife, 10. DOI
The Role of Spatial Self-Organization in the Design of Agroforestry Systems (2020) Tzuk, O., Uecker, H., and Meron, E. PLOS ONE, 15(7). DOI
Period Doubling as an Indicator for Ecosystem Sensitivity to Climate Extremes (2019) Tzuk, O., et al. Scientific Reports, 9(1), 19577. DOI
Interplay between Exogenous and Endogenous Factors in Seasonal Vegetation Oscillations (2019) Tzuk, O., et al. Scientific Reports, 9(1), 354. DOI
Front Instabilities Can Reverse Desertification (2019) Fernandez-Oto, C., Tzuk, O., and Meron, E. Phys. Rev. Lett., 122(4). DOI
Continuum Modeling of Discrete Plant Communities (2019) Meron, E., et al. Mathematics, 7(10). DOI
Wavelength Selection beyond Turing (2017) Zelnik, Y. R. and Tzuk, O. Eur. Phys. J. Spec. Top., 226(9). DOI
Discovery of Fairy Circles in Australia Supports Self-Organization Theory (2016) Getzin, S., et al. Proc Natl Acad Sci USA, 113(13). DOI






Lecturer | 2020 – Oct 2024 Tel-Aviv University and Afeka College of Engineering Operations research, stochastic processes, data mining, ML, and business analytics.
Lecturer | 2019 – Present Open University, Israel Physics courses and introduction to astronomy.
Teaching Assistant | 2014 – 2019 Ben-Gurion University of the Negev Mathematical Methods, Quantum Physics, and fundamental physics.






Co-Founder & Chair | 2023 – Present Spirit Tech Hub Israel Convening stakeholders around technologies for mental well-being and contemplative science.
Co-Founder & Chief Editor | 2014 – 2019 humanist.co.il An online magazine and community hub for the secular humanist community in Israel.






Blue Sky Leaders Certificate Program | 2026 California Institute of Integral Studies (CIIS) Conscious Leadership, systems thinking, and regenerative design.
Facilitators of Dialogue Training Course | 2025 The School for Peace (SFP) Training in facilitation of Jewish-Arab dialogue and conflict transformation.






Core: Algorithm development, numerical analysis, mathematical modeling, data science, complex networks, graph ML, graph databases, EEG pipelines.
Software: Python, MATLAB, Pandas, Scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PyTorch, Neo4j, MNE-Python, EEGLAB.
Languages: Hebrew (Native), English (Fluent), Italian (Fluent).

title: Sketchnotes
text:
Footnotes


Disclaimer: the primary audience for these notes is myself, and I make no guarantees about accuracy or quality of the content inside.↩︎

title: Gallery Cheatsheet
text:
These notes accompany my templates for posting different types of visual content:
They detail commonly used processes, relevant tools, and notes on design and accessibility considerations.
This page was last compiled on November 07, 2022.

title: Gallery Cheatsheet
text:
Web Design

Quarto






TipAdd parameterised text using params: field





Set values in the params: field of the YAML header.
This is an inline code chunk for conditional inclusion of text:
`r if(params$interactive){"This sketchnote is interactive. Try clicking around various parts of the image"}`
Toggle the </> code menu to see the R code chunk params-prep used to prepare variables used in text about the sketchnote.









NoteAll about the YAML





Include these values every time:
title: "Sketchnote Page"
subtitle: "for visual summaries, illustrations, etc."
category: "illustrations/visual-summaries/conceptual-maps/dataviz/misc"
date: 2022-11-01
These are used to generate text:
params:
  drawing_tool: "notability/concepts" ## must be in _params-dict.R ##
  date_drawn: 2022-11-01
  draw_type: "illustration/sketchnote" ## pick one
  interactive: TRUE ## if SVG contains any links or interactivity
  main_alt_text: "" ## write this using the relevant alt-text strategy ##
Include only if you want to override the default values gallery/_metadata.yml:

just save images/objects using the correct name (featured.*, main.*)
add any resources embedded using HTML tags to the resources: list. If not, they won’t get copied into the output directory.

image: "images/featured.png"
image-alt: "A hand-drawn illustration/sketchnote described in this article"
resources:
  - "images/main.svg"
Specific to template posts:
format:
  html:
    code-tools: true





SVG






TipAdd anchors (links) nodes to to .svg using Inkscape






Export sketch from ipad to images/ipad.png or images/ipad.svg (depending on app support)
Start a new Inkscape document and import the sketch.
Crop the image to remove excess white space (i.e. if iPad export was A4): how-to
Resize the canvas to fit: File > Document Properties > Fit Page to Selection
Convert image Object to Pattern
Divide image into anchor regions by adding a line (b) or shape, add the image pattern to the selection (s), and applying Path > Division (⌘/): tutorial
Right click divided region and select Create anchors. Add URL to href field, and set target=_blank
Inspect XML tree to check anchor nodes (optional)
Save graphic to images/feature.svg




Screenshot of inkscape menu dialogues and options for adding anchor











TipHTML Snippet for embedding SVG using <object>





The default snippet assumes the sketchnote/svg is named main.svg
To customise the display or caption of the sketchnote/illustration, or to display multiple SVGs, modify and include the snippet below in place of the {{< include ../../_snippet-main-svg.md >}} shortcode .

.column-page class is predefined by Quarto theme1
<figure> tag provides support for a <figcaption>2
<object> embeds the SVG, with .sketchnote class defined in styles.css specifying type="image/svg+xml" and other custom attributes

:::{.column-page}
<figure>
  <object data="images/main.svg" class="sketchnote"> </object>
  <figcaption style="text-align: right">Cynthia Huang © `r format(ddate, "%Y")`, [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)</figcaption>
</figure>
:::

Description of `r dtype`:

> `r params$main_alt_text`

`r if(params$interactive) {"Links embedded in the graphic:"}`









NoteIncluding interactive SVGs





There are four ways to include SVG in Web pages:

as an <img> element. This is what ![](sketchnote.svg) will render as and essentially converts the SVG into a raster image (which means no child element interactions).
as a CSS background. Haven’t tried this; seems to be recommended for decorative images.
Inline SVG. Also haven’t tried this; would likely preclude GUI editing of the SVG so probably only suitable for simple vector graphics (which don’t have the complicated pattern elements of sketchnotes)
as an application embedded via <object> element. This preserves any links embedded inside the SVG, and supports interfacing between the parent document and the SVG object via SVG DOM (which I don’t yet understand how to use).

Reference Resources:

Chapter 1. Using SVG in Web Pages of Modern SVG by Amelia Bellamy-Royds and Kurt Cagle for extended discussion
Using SVG by Chris Coyier on CSS-tricks for short tutorial

2022-11-02: I picked embedding via the <object> element because it was the only one where hyperlinks I inserted into the .svg using Inkspace still worked. I plan to experiment with more advanced interactivity in the future.

title: Gallery Cheatsheet
text:
Web Accessibility






WarningNotes on SVG and Sketchnote Accessibility





About SVGs and alt-text attributes:

SVG is not an image, it is a graphics-document (defined using XML)
alt-text for the sketchnote is defined in params: main_alt_text: using the strategies detailed below
aria-label or aria-labelledby attributes are recommended for objects.3
Fallback content (inside <object> tags) is only rendered when the object embedding is not successful, so is not a suitable place to put text for accessibility 4
including <title> and <desc> directly in the SVG is often recommended but only suitable for inline SVG.5

About sketchnote/data-viz accessibility:

if the SVG is interactive you can make it screen reader traversable… but it’s complicated and involves ordering and naming nodes appropriately, amongst other things. One would need to adapt these instructions for creating an Accessible Graph.
an option for 301 level WCAG conformance offered by Nitya Narasimhan in this discussion thread started by Megan Sullivan is turning the graphic into a “interactive information guide”

Resources List:

Accessible SVGs - University of Melbourne for background context and simple code exampes
Accessible SVGs by Heather Migliorisi for more extended discussion and complex code examples
Sketch Note Accessibility by Saara Kamppari-Miller for example of extending alt-text accessibility with a Talk-Through Video which describes the emergent information structure of a sketchnote.
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 Success Criterion 1.1.1: Non-text Content for comprehensive discussion and detailed alt-text techniques and scenarios.6

2022-11-02: At the moment, all alt-text is provided below the SVG figure. I don’t think this is optimal for screen-readers but embedding alt-text is complicated and beyond me right now.7 My approach will likely evolve as I learn more html/css/svg concepts.




Alt Text Strategies






TipAlt Text Strategy for Conceptual Illustrations





Use some or all of the following elements to describe the purpose, content and “flow” of the illustration. The description can be more comprehensive compared to a sketchnote.

Illustration Type:

cartoon
schematic
illlustration
(line) plot
visual representations

Description of visual elements with position context:

On the left…
A row of…
Left panel… Right panel…
The progression goes from…

Text quotes and labels in context:

Text above… quotes Jenny Bryan: “Collaboration is…”
Text above the laptop reads: “Plan on it.”
several anchors, each labeled “Commit”
a cape that reads “code hero”…

Clarification of key message or takeaway

The point being: …
Main text:


Strategy and examples adapted from Allison Horst’s Stats Illustrations









TipAlt Text Strategy for Visual Summaries





Follow this recipe for image descriptions which provide context and some key details about the sketchnote. Remember the point isn’t to share every visual and text element of the sketchnote in the alt-text, but to describe the function provided by the sketchnote.

Visual Context: black and white {digital/hand-drawn} visual notes
Content Context: summarising {title of source media or event}
Key takeaway: {key quote or note}
Prominent visual element: {any signature elements for which visual description should be included}

Adapted from Sketch Note Accessibility by Saara Kamppari-Miller

title: About
text:
I am a physicist and complex-systems researcher currently expanding my work into consciousness science. I completed my B.Sc. in Astronomy & Astrophysics at the University of Bologna, followed by an M.Sc. in Gravitation and Astroparticle Physics (GRAPPA) at the University of Amsterdam.
I then moved into the physics of complex systems at Ben-Gurion University, where my Ph.D. focused on pattern formation and eco-hydrological dynamics in semi-arid ecosystems — studying how dryland vegetation self-organizes as a buffer against aridity and climate stress. Afterward, I worked on spreading phenomena on complex networks during a postdoctoral position at Tel Aviv University.
In between, I spent several years in industry as a data scientist, building knowledge graph systems and LLM-based agents across domains ranging from precision agriculture to psychophysiological health.
My current research moves between environmental complex systems and contemplative neuroscience. I am a postdoctoral fellow at the Contemplative Neuroscience and Neurophenomenology Lab at the University of Haifa, where I study EEG time-series, predictive processing, and altered states of consciousness. In parallel, I hold a postdoctoral position in Yair Mau’s Eco-Hydrology Lab at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem.
Outside the lab, I co-founded Spirit Tech Hub Israel, a community connecting researchers, developers, and entrepreneurs working at the intersection of technology, consciousness, and human flourishing.
Across these fields, my interest remains constant: understanding how complex systems — ecological, cognitive, and social — self-organize, adapt, and transform.
